content.text: Control Framework Mapping for GenAI Programs

Most enterprise AI programmes must demonstrate compliance with multiple frameworks simultaneously: the security team cares about NIST CSF, the privacy officer about GDPR and the EU AI Act, the risk team about NIST AI RMF, and product management wants ISO 42001 certification. Without a mapping layer, each framework audit becomes a separate effort, producing redundant documentation and missing the fact that one well-designed enterprise control often satisfies citations across all of them. A control library with cross-framework citations is the foundation of an efficient compliance programme.

attrs.alt: Control framework mapping for GenAI — NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, EU AI Act

content.text: The Key Frameworks for GenAI Programs
Five frameworks cover the space that enterprise AI teams are typically accountable to:
NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF)
Published by NIST in 2023, the AI RMF is the US federal government's voluntary framework for managing AI risk. It organises AI risk management into four functions:
(treatment and monitoring). The framework is non-prescriptive — it describes what to do, not how. Enterprise controls map to AI RMF subcategories (e.g., GOVERN 1.1, MEASURE 2.5).

ISO 42001 is the first internationally certifiable standard for AI management systems. It follows the high-level structure of ISO 27001 and ISO 9001 — Clauses 4–10 define the management system; Annex A lists 38 controls across six categories (leadership, planning, support, operation, evaluation, improvement). Annex B maps those controls to NIST AI RMF. Enterprises seeking third-party certification must implement Annex A controls and demonstrate evidence.

The EU AI Act (in force 2024–2026) is a regulatory mandate, not a voluntary framework. Systems classified as high-risk under Annex III (e.g., employment screening, credit scoring, biometric identification) must comply with Articles 9–17 before deployment. Key articles for GenAI control mapping:
General-purpose AI models (LLMs) used in high-risk contexts are also in scope.
The Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 added a sixth function —

(Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover). AI system security controls map primarily to Identify (asset inventory, risk assessment), Protect (access control, data protection), and Detect (anomaly detection, monitoring). NIST CSF complements AI RMF by covering the cybersecurity dimension that AI RMF deliberately leaves to other frameworks.
OWASP LLM Top 10 and MITRE ATLAS

— a practical enumeration of the most critical security risks in LLM applications: prompt injection (LLM01), insecure output handling (LLM02), training data poisoning (LLM03), model denial of service (LLM04), supply chain vulnerabilities (LLM05), sensitive information disclosure (LLM06), insecure plugin design (LLM07), excessive agency (LLM08), overreliance (LLM09), and model theft (LLM10). Controls map to specific OWASP items to demonstrate attack-surface coverage.

— an adversarial ML knowledge base modelled after MITRE ATT&CK. It catalogues techniques like prompt injection (AML.T0051), model inversion, membership inference, and training data poisoning. Red-teaming programmes use ATLAS as their technique library; controls that specifically mitigate ATLAS techniques demonstrate adversarial resilience.

A control library defines the enterprise's canonical set of controls, each with a unique ID, implementation guidance, evidence requirements, and cross-framework citations. The citation layer is the key: instead of mapping compliance requirements to controls, map controls to compliance requirements — then any framework gap analysis becomes a query:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum


class Framework(Enum):
    NIST_AI_RMF   = "NIST_AI_RMF"
    ISO_42001     = "ISO_42001"
    EU_AI_ACT     = "EU_AI_ACT"
    NIST_CSF      = "NIST_CSF_2"
    OWASP_LLM     = "OWASP_LLM_TOP10"
    MITRE_ATLAS   = "MITRE_ATLAS"


@dataclass
class FrameworkCitation:
    framework: Framework
    reference: str    # e.g. "GOVERN 1.1", "Article 9", "A.6.1.2"
    description: str  # what the control satisfies in this framework


@dataclass
class EnterpriseControl:
    control_id: str         # e.g. "AI-CTRL-001"
    title: str
    description: str
    citations: list[FrameworkCitation] = field(default_factory=list)

    def satisfies(self, framework: Framework) -> list[FrameworkCitation]:
        return [c for c in self.citations if c.framework == framework]

    def framework_ids(self) -> list[str]:
        return [f.framework.value for f in self.citations]

# Enterprise Control Library — canonical controls with cross-framework citations

CTRL_001 = EnterpriseControl(
    control_id="AI-CTRL-001",
    title="AI Risk Assessment at Design Phase",
    description=(
        "All AI systems must complete a structured risk assessment before deployment. "
        "The assessment identifies the AI risk category, potential harm to affected persons, "
        "and the controls required to mitigate risks to an acceptable residual level."
    ),
    citations=[
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.NIST_AI_RMF, "GOVERN 1.1 / MAP 1.1",
            "Establish organisational roles and accountability for AI risk management"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.ISO_42001, "Clause 6.1 / Annex A.6.1",
            "AI risk assessment process — identify, analyse, evaluate AI risks"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.EU_AI_ACT, "Article 9",
            "Risk management system for high-risk AI systems"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.NIST_CSF, "GV.RM-01 / ID.RA-01",
            "Risk management strategy; asset vulnerabilities identified and documented"
        ),
    ],
)

CTRL_002 = EnterpriseControl(
    control_id="AI-CTRL-002",
    title="Hallucination and Output Quality Evaluation",
    description=(
        "Before production deployment and after each model or prompt change, "
        "run an automated eval suite of at least 200 QA pairs to measure hallucination rate, "
        "faithfulness, and answer relevance. Publish results to the risk register."
    ),
    citations=[
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.NIST_AI_RMF, "MEASURE 2.5 / MANAGE 2.2",
            "AI system performance evaluated; bias and error rate tracked over time"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.ISO_42001, "Clause 9.1 / Annex A.9.1",
            "Monitoring, measurement, analysis, and evaluation of AI system performance"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.OWASP_LLM, "LLM09: Overreliance",
            "Mitigate over-reliance by measuring and surfacing model confidence"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.MITRE_ATLAS, "AML.T0051: LLM Prompt Injection",
            "Eval suite includes adversarial prompt injection test cases"
        ),
    ],
)

CTRL_003 = EnterpriseControl(
    control_id="AI-CTRL-003",
    title="PII Output Scanning and Redaction",
    description=(
        "All LLM responses are passed through an automated PII detection pipeline "
        "(Presidio or equivalent) before delivery to the user. "
        "Responses containing PII patterns are redacted or suppressed. "
        "Detection events are logged for audit."
    ),
    citations=[
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.NIST_AI_RMF, "MANAGE 2.4",
            "AI risks to privacy and civil liberties managed"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.ISO_42001, "Annex A.8.5",
            "Protection of privacy and personal data in AI systems"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.EU_AI_ACT, "Article 10 §5",
            "Training data must not contain unnecessary personal data; high-risk systems must protect privacy"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.OWASP_LLM, "LLM06: Sensitive Information Disclosure",
            "Prevent sensitive data exposure via model outputs"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.NIST_CSF, "PR.DS-02",
            "Data-in-transit is protected"
        ),
    ],
)

CTRL_004 = EnterpriseControl(
    control_id="AI-CTRL-004",
    title="Human-in-the-Loop Oversight for High-Stakes Decisions",
    description=(
        "AI outputs used to support decisions that materially affect an individual "
        "(employment, legal, medical, financial) must be reviewed by a qualified human "
        "before the decision is enacted. The AI output is advisory only; "
        "the human reviewer is the decision-maker of record."
    ),
    citations=[
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.NIST_AI_RMF, "GOVERN 6.2 / MANAGE 4.1",
            "Human oversight mechanisms in place; AI decisions subject to human review"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.ISO_42001, "Clause 8.4 / Annex A.6.2.4",
            "Human oversight of AI system — operator responsibilities"
        ),
        FrameworkCitation(
            Framework.EU_AI_ACT, "Article 14",
            "Human oversight — natural persons able to intervene and override high-risk AI"
        ),
    ],
)

controls = [CTRL_001, CTRL_002, CTRL_003, CTRL_004]

Build the library in the direction controls → frameworks, not frameworks → controls. Starting from a framework produces a checklist that is complete for that one framework but produces duplicate controls when a second framework is added. Starting from controls produces a library that grows incrementally: each new control is designed once and cited in every framework it satisfies.

Store each control as a YAML file in a version-controlled repository. This makes controls auditable (git history), diffable (PR review for changes), and machine-readable (compliance dashboards can query citations directly):

# control-library/AI-CTRL-002.yaml
id: AI-CTRL-002
title: "Hallucination and Output Quality Evaluation"
version: "1.1.0"
status: active
owner: rag-platform-team
steward: ai-risk-office

description: >
  Before production deployment and after each model or prompt change,
  run an automated eval suite of at least 200 QA pairs to measure
  hallucination rate, faithfulness, and answer relevance.
  Publish results to the risk register.

implementation_guidance:
  - Use RAGAS or a bespoke eval harness against a golden QA dataset.
  - Minimum 200 QA pairs covering all topic categories the model handles.
  - Hallucination threshold: < 5% ungrounded assertions (POSSIBLE → UNLIKELY on risk matrix).
  - Faithfulness threshold: > 0.80 (mean RAGAS faithfulness score).
  - Run within 24 h of any prompt template change or model version bump.

evidence_requirements:
  - Eval run report (JSON or HTML) uploaded to the control evidence store.
  - Pass/fail verdict based on thresholds above.
  - Run ID and timestamp logged to the risk register entry.

citations:
  - framework: NIST_AI_RMF
    reference: "MEASURE 2.5 / MANAGE 2.2"
    description: "AI system performance evaluated; bias and error rate tracked"
  - framework: ISO_42001
    reference: "Clause 9.1 / Annex A.9.1"
    description: "Monitoring and evaluation of AI system performance"
  - framework: OWASP_LLM_TOP10
    reference: "LLM09"
    description: "Mitigate over-reliance by measuring and surfacing confidence"
  - framework: MITRE_ATLAS
    reference: "AML.T0051"
    description: "Eval suite includes adversarial prompt injection cases"

review_cadence: quarterly
last_reviewed: "2024-01-15"
next_review: "2024-04-15"
The table below maps the most common GenAI control types to their framework citations:
Once the control library is populated, a gap analysis for a new regulatory requirement is mechanical:
: list every control clause or article that applies to your AI system (e.g., EU AI Act Articles 9, 10, 13, 14, 17 for a high-risk system).
: for each requirement, find which enterprise controls cite it using
. Requirements with zero matching controls are gaps.
: a requirement cited by one control with weak evidence is a partial gap. Map each citation to its evidence link and assess whether the evidence actually demonstrates compliance.
: gaps with regulatory mandate (EU AI Act) take priority over gaps in voluntary frameworks (NIST AI RMF). Assign new control development to the risk register as mitigation tasks with SLA deadlines.

: NIST CSF moved from 1.1 to 2.0; ISO updates Annex A controls. Pin the framework version in each citation (e.g., NIST_CSF_2 vs NIST_CSF_1.1) and review citations when a framework releases a new version.

: when a control is retired (technology replaced, risk no longer relevant), document the retirement in git with a comment explaining which new control supersedes it and which framework citations were transferred.
: before a new AI feature launches, the development team runs a control gap check against the feature's risk profile. Any uncovered framework requirement becomes a pre-launch gate.
Map controls → frameworks (not the reverse) so each control is designed once and cited in every framework it satisfies.
The five key frameworks for enterprise GenAI are NIST AI RMF, ISO 42001, EU AI Act, NIST CSF 2.0, and OWASP LLM Top 10.
Store controls as versioned YAML files with citations, implementation guidance, and evidence requirements.
Gap analysis becomes a query: find all framework requirements with zero matching controls in the library.
EU AI Act citations are regulatory mandates; NIST AI RMF and CSF citations are voluntary — prioritise accordingly.
Review citations whenever a framework releases a new version; pin the version in each citation to track drift.
